경찰 언론 민주당 정치인들이 종북 주사파 조선족 삼합회 한국 전국구 카르텔과 오래전 부터 물 밑에서 물질적 내물 부정 부패로 상호 공조 결탁 공생  야합 하고  범죄 조직들 뒤를 봐주고 보호 하고 있는 현실 입니다.

종북 주사파들과 한국 일본 카르텔이, 한국 일본 동남아 75년 침투 뒤에서 모든 것을 장악하고 배후에서 조종하고 있습니다.

현실 조차 인지 하지 못하는 미국. 유럽. 영국 정부는 정말 답답합니다. 무책임 하게 방어 대책 조차 하지 않고 있습니다. 경재 돈이 문제가 아니라. 한국 일본 국가를 침략 당했습니다. 일본. 한국을 정복 하는 시간은 75년 입니다. 다음 타켓은 30~50년 이내 안에 간첩 작전 세력들이 미국 유럽 체제를 분열 해체 시켜 정치. 군부. 행정부. 기업들. 언론 모두 매수된 자신들의 세력들로 전원 세력 교체. 정권 교체. 세대 교체. 군부 장악 배후에서 북한 한국 일본 종북 주사파들이 수십만명 차명 명의로 숨겨둔 3000조원 이상의 자금력. 기술력. 교류. 투자. 협력. 단합. 동맹 내세워 단합 교류 위장하고 침투해 본심을 드러내고 흡수 야합 하게 될것입니다 본색을 드러내는 수십년 후에는 그때는 이미 늦었습니다 끝입니다. 미국 유럽 백성들은 공산당 간첩들과 기업들의 자금 아래서 지배 공급 탄압 받는 공산당 간첩들의 소굴이 될것입니다. 북한 한국 일본은 정체와 목적을 감추고 변장 하고 있습니다 

유럽 미국 보다 정치적 기술이 몇수 위에 있습니다 속지 마세요! 현재 이득을 보지 말고 넓게 멀리 계산 하세요. 북한 한국 일본 동남아 정권 배후에서 누가 지배 하고 있습니까? 미국 유럽은 순진하고 단순하고 구식 입니다.
한국 일본 북한 깡패 양아치 국가 입니다. 수박 처럼 겉과 속이 다릅니다. 속지 마세요! 미국은, 종교 및 돈 기술력 자금을 방패 공격 침투 정치적 목적 수단으로 정치적 군사적 약화 시켜, 부정 부패 물질 만능 이용하는 매수 하려는 고도의 정치적 수법 수단 입니다 그들은 개인이 아니라 전체 공동 사상으로 주변 국가들 침투해 세력을 장악하고 넓혀 왔습니다. 바로 주변 국가들 장악 정복 야심에 미쳐 있습니다. 미국은, 한국 북한 일본 동남아 국가들을 경계 이상의 분리 단절 제거 군사적 조취 작전을 취해야 합니다.

그들을 막을 시간이 없습니다, 현실 정치 체제는 이미 영혼을 악마에게 속아서 팔았습니다. 이미 늦었을 수도 있습니다. 종북 주사파 그들의 변장 둔갑 책략 술책 수에 미국. 유럽과 세계 국가들은 영혼을 팔았습니다. 서로 정치적 군사적 경재적 동맹 이라는 속임수 늪에 빠져 있습니다. 동맹국 이라는 거짓 말에 서로 갈라 놓을수 없을 만큼 떼어 놓지 못할 만큼, 오랜 시간 동안 마인드 컨트롤 체면에 걸려 있습니다. 

미국 유럽은 정신 차리세요!

북한 한국 일본 동남아 국가들에서 대략 75년 동안 침투해 모두 배후에서 그들 세력들이 90% 장악

This video is a half-baked explanation that creates more questions than it actually answers. 
The creation and issuance of money, per se, only causes inflation if there is more money chasing too few goods. The question is: why does this happen in the first place? If money creation were restored to government there would be no need for inflation.

text:
1. The official inflation rate reported by the Consumer Price Index (CPI) significantly understates the actual inflation experienced by consumers. The CPI tracks the price of a "basket of goods and services" intended to represent average consumer spending, but the methodology has been altered over time in ways that hide the true effects of inflation.

2. In the 1980s, the Federal Reserve began changing the CPI calculation to allow for substitution of cheaper, lower-quality alternatives when prices of certain goods rise faster than others. This means the CPI no longer accurately tracks the cost of maintaining a constant standard of living, as it was originally intended to do.

3. If the CPI were calculated using the same methodology as in the 1980s, the current inflation rate in the United States would be around 18% - nearly double the 9.1% officially reported for June 2022. This would be even higher than the worst periods of the late 1970s/early 1980s inflation crisis.

4. The Federal Reserve's motivation for altering the CPI calculation method may be more sinister than simply hiding their own culpability in the current economic turmoil. It could be to protect their preferred mechanisms for manipulating the economy, such as monetary policy tools.

5. The massive gap between the true, unacknowledged inflation rate and the official CPI is so severe that it could potentially lead to scenarios akin to the hyperinflation experienced in countries like Zimbabwe, where prices can literally change before a consumer reaches the checkout line.

text:
Tired of the "automation won't replace humans, but work with them" nonsense. Entry-level today, mid/late career tomorrow, C-suite the next day. Starting with the great decoupling in the 1970s, human labor has been and will continue trending toward zero. Think EXPONENTIALLY, not LINEARLY. Automation and robotics will necessarily replace human workers due to the current economic system. Today, AI is the WORST it will ever be going forward. It DOESN'T MATTER if AI "creates" 200 million new jobs...AI will solve and execute these new "jobs" much faster than humans will be able to adapt, learn, and fill them. Capital/employee model-it worked great...for quite a while. However, now companies that automate lower their costs, become more competitive. Those that keep higher-cost human labor necessarily fall behind and will go out of business. This creates a system where replacing people with machines is not just likely, but necessary for company survival. There is a way to deal with this humanely, but it would require a full revamp of the social contract and our concept of work. Even capitalists will want this change when unemployment reaches a tipping point and there’s no one left with money to buy the crap they’re selling.

I know, LLMs are leveling off, "what about the energy?," blah, blah, blah. Do not conflate LLMs with AI. LLMs are no more AI than vacuum tubes are computers or AOL dial-up is the internet. So, LLMs are leveling off. History shows technological advance is not just one sigmoid curve. It's a series of sigmoid curves with each successive one reaching new capabilities and new heights. To think that after 200,000 years, this pattern is all the sudden going to stop is absurd. I also understand there will be new jobs for humans created, it will go in this pattern: Automation creates 5 new jobs this year, lays off 50; creates 5 new jobs next year, lays off 900; creates 5 new jobs the following year, lays off 15,000 etc. In the macro economic picture, do you not see this as an issue? I also understand there is more opportunity for entrepreneurs. Again, from the macro perspective there is a MINORITY of people that have the entrepreneurial ability/spirit. It takes that, and a strong majority of people don't have it.

Reasoning AI Is the Next Big Leap. The future of AI is not just about generating answers, but about “reasoning” breaking down complex problems step-by-step, more like a human brain. Breakthroughs like DeepSeek’s open-source reasoning models are already being incorporated into platforms like GPT and xAI, marking a shift toward more advanced, thoughtful AI. Think EXPONENTIALLY, not LINEARLY.

There’s a persistent and misleading narrative circulating often perpetuated by short term goal-driven institutions like politicians, universities, and tech company executives, that the future of work is secure. The claim goes: “You just need to learn how to work with AI,” and humans collaborating with AI will outperform either one alone. But recent research, including a landmark study from the University of Virginia, tells a different story. In diagnosing medical conditions, AI alone achieved far greater accuracy than either human doctors or even doctors assisted by AI. The median diagnostic accuracy for the docs using Chat GPT Plus was 76.3%, while the results for the physicians using conventional approaches was 73.7%. Sounds like “a win” at first. However, Chat GPT Plus alone achieved a median diagnostic accuracy of more than 92%. In other words, the comforting idea that humans will always be essential partners to machines is crumbling. In many fields, AI is not just a tool, it is the superior performer. This has staggering implications for the future of work, especially to the often used solution of prior technological displacement that go “You just need to be retrained.” To the group advocating the "retraining solution," I pose to you this: You have a medical doctor, just finished 8 years of higher education, a low-paid 4-year internship/residency, is hundreds of thousands of dollars in debt, and just got outperformed by automation; what would you have this doctor retrained into that will offer a secure job?

The result of this, since the United States' moronic system is based on taxing employees for about 70% of all tax revenue, this will further exacerbate the problem. Tax needs to be based on what's going on now and in the future not what worked 75 years ago. Oh I know it's so evil to think, but we might need to tax the companies who are doing the automation. Even if you do that, it's a win-win. For instance say a company had 1,000 employees in automated away down to 100. 1,000 employees paid federal taxes, including payroll tax, of about 20 million a year. Now the 100 people pay about 2 million a year. However, by automating, the company sees two things: they are no longer paying approximately 60 million in wages and their share of payroll tax, AND since nobody automates to lose money they also have higher profits. What's so wrong about taxing that company for the $60 million dollars they don't have to pay anymore and a fair percentage of their additional profits they now make through the automation. Not all the additional profits, but some of them. If you do that there's a higher tax revenue and more profits for the company. That's a win-win for the government and for businesses. If humanity, as a whole, weren't so stupid, we would just adjust as tech progresses to where EVERYONE benefits somewhat from each stage and just move smoothly into the post-labor economy.

To understand this shift, consider the historical role of the horse and oxen. They were once essential to the economy as sources of power for agriculture, transport, and industry. Technologies like the horse collar and the steel plow dramatically increased their output, making them even more valuable. But then came the steam engine and, later, the internal combustion engine. These machines didn’t just help the horse, but outperformed it entirely. Trains, tractors, and trucks could do the job ten, even a hundred times faster and more powerfully. And from that moment forward, the horse was no longer critical to economic output. It was, in essence, unemployed.

This is what AI and robotics are doing to human labor. They are not simply replacing the repetitive or the physical; they are increasingly outperforming humans in cognition, strategy, design, and communication. And just like the horse, our value in the economic system will diminish, not from lack of talent or effort, but because machines will do the job better. That’s why we need to start thinking not in terms of retraining, but about restructuring society itself for a world where human labor is no longer essential to production. When the very essence of what makes an entity useful to the current economy is superseded (be that strength, endurance, dexterity, analysis, creativity, empathy, intelligence, persuasion as well as many other qualities) that entity becomes irrelevant to economic output.

Potential solution. Decentralized AI Ownership: A Bridge Toward Post-Scarcity. While Universal Basic Income (UBI) may serve as an initial economic stopgap, bridging today’s economy with tomorrow’s abundance consider an even more equitable and long-term solution: Decentralizing the ownership of artificial intelligence itself. 

Rather than concentrating the power of AI in the hands of a few corporations or governments, envision a model where AI entities are open-source and collectively owned. This could take the form of assigning each person a digital "share" of an AI system, not unlike a stakeholder’s share in a company. As the AI produces value, whether through intellectual labor, creative output, or autonomous economic participation, those shares would generate dividends distributed directly to individuals. 

This model would ensure that as AI becomes the dominant productive force in society, the wealth it creates is shared universally, not monopolized. It aligns with the principle that the fruits of public knowledge made possible by centuries of human collaboration should benefit everyone, not just a few early adopters or private entities. In practice, this could look like: 

- AI systems running Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) that distribute profits algorithmically.
- Smart contracts ensuring transparent, equitable income flows based on collective stake.
- Global “AI cooperatives” where users vote on how AI is used, improved, and deployed.
- Universal basic compute.
 
If AI becomes the primary driver of economic output, then equitable access to its benefits isn’t just fair, it’s necessary to avoid deepening inequality and instability. It provides a scalable, technologically aligned extension to UBI, ensuring people not only have income, but true economic participation in the age of intelligent machines. 

Ultimately, decentralized AI ownership may be the last transition phase before full post-scarcity, when advanced technologies like universal nanofactories or matter compilers render even economic structures obsolete. Until then, however, models like this offer a way to reclaim agency in a world where production no longer requires human hands, and where wealth can, for the first time in history, truly be universal.

text:
“Some people just want to see the world burn.”

No, they just don’t fit our script.
That line isn’t wisdom, it’s a shortcut. A lazy excuse to dehumanize anyone who doesn’t play along. Because the moment we call someone a monster, we no longer have to ask what made them one.
Truth is people don’t snap for no reason. They’re pushed. Ignored. Cornered. Rewritten by a system that breaks them first and judges them later.
And we? We don’t question it.
We scroll.
We consume.
We decide.
30 seconds. 60 seconds. A minute and a half and suddenly we are the judge of good and evil. No context. No depth. Just whatever narrative was packaged clean enough for our attention span.
And who’s writing that script?

All those giant companies who accumulate 24 hours of human routine into content creation.

These aren’t just platforms, they’re architects of perception.
They don’t show us the world. They filter it, bend it, and sell it back to us optimized for outrage, addiction, and control.

They don’t need chains.
They’ve got algorithms.
And now that people are starting to notice, starting to question, to resist, that’s when things get ugly. Because power doesn’t give up control peacefully. It divides. It distracts. It turns people against each other while sitting comfortably at the top.
“Truth is dangerous.” Not because it destroys people.
But because it exposes who’s been controlling them.
So they create noise. Conflict. Labels. Sides.
Anything to keep us busy fighting each other…
instead of looking up.
This “new order”? This “balance”?
It’s not natural. It’s engineered.
And the scariest part?

We formally request the full integration of China ROM features, visuals, and physics-based animations into Global builds. Our objective is to achieve complete UI parity and a seamless
(1)👻AI Interactive Companion 
is a highly customizable digital partner that stays on your home screen as a responsive widget, reflecting system health through its "energy levels" and battery-synced animations. It seamlessly integrates with existing widgets or stands alone, allowing you to change its entire theme and style to match your personal aesthetic while managing all on-device AI tasks
(2) 💻 The Office Kit introduces "Origin Plugins,
 a deep integration layer that applies the full OriginOS design language and interactive skin over Windows and Linux for a premium, unified aesthetic. Crucially, it serves as a fail-safe; if Windows suffers a Blue Screen or system failure, the Kit instantly activates an integrated Android-based PC backup environment. This secondary OS hosts your synced files and active workspace, allowing you to continue your tasks without interruption even when the primary desktop OS fails
(3)🖌️ Super Icons (Icon Enlargement)
 OriginOS allows users to resize app icons using four different dimensional presets. To ensure a cohesive aesthetic, this "Super Icon" functionality is supported by all available Theme Icon Packs
(4) 🧊GridForge Seamless Block Architecture
GridForge allows users to visually merge enlarged icons and widgets into a single, gapless "puzzle" block, eliminating all dividing lines and padding. While the elements appear as one unified aesthetic unit, they maintain their independent functional performance for a cleaner, high-efficiency home screen experience
(5)🎨 AI Wallpapers
Use Little Blue and Gemini to generate art from text prompts or transform existing photos into wallpapers
(6)🫆 Custom fingerprint 
This feature allows users to personalize their fingerprint scanner by using custom PNG images or photos instead of standard emojis. It offers full creative freedom to replace the default biometric icon with any transparent graphic or personal image for a truly unique lock screen experience
(7)🎭Deep system theming 
implements adaptive custom designs that reshape the entire UI, from Control Center toggles to system menus. Rather than simple color shifts, it integrates unique textures like glassmorphism to ensure the entire interface functions as a singular, cohesive masterpiece
(8)🇨🇳 OriginOS China Features (Requesting for Global)

text:
I think this video raises some legitimate concerns, but the overall narrative feels a bit stronger than the available evidence really supports.

Most of the individual points are clearly framed as rumors or supply-chain reports, which is appropriate. Short-term supply skew toward lower-VRAM SKUs, pricing pressure on higher-end cards, and NVIDIA’s incentives to prioritize AI datacenter products are all plausible and worth discussing. None of that strikes me as fabricated.

Where I think the argument overreaches is in the synthesis. A possible Q1 2026 production mix shift doesn’t by itself amount to “RIP high-end GPUs.” NVIDIA has a long history of limited halo-product volumes early in cycles, and the video itself acknowledges that production breakdowns aren’t public and the scale of the change is uncertain. That uncertainty matters.

There’s also a bit of rumor-stacking going on: memory shortages, AI margins, VRAM binning, pricing spikes, connector failures, and foundry diversification are all real or plausible issues, but treating them as converging proof of an irreversible trend feels premature. The pessimistic framing does more work than the data requires.

In short, I see this less as misinformation and more as sentiment journalism aimed at a frustrated enthusiast audience. The underlying concerns are reasonable; the conclusion just seems overconfident given how cyclical and opaque GPU markets actually are. I’d be very curious to revisit the same thesis in six or twelve months with harder numbers on actual shipment volumes and SKU mix.

Don't do "Linux research", because with this many options out there, you'll never even get started - its pure choice paralysis.

Just pick some older 4GB+ RAM hardware, or buy something like a "Beelink SER5" which has very affordable options such as "6/8 Core 16GB/500GB", and give it a go.
DON'T dual-boot, because Windows nowadays "accidentally" likes to kill Linux installs during its updates.

Also, don't ever enter the terminal, that "weird black window of doom", for any installation or configuration, because most of what a casual Linux desktop user needs can be had or configured through common menus that don't make you rethink your live-choices while eroding your install.
Most importantly, use the "Software Manager" for your app installs, and if you can't find what you are looking for then try an alternative - everything you can select there has been tested, is easily installed, and will be kept up-to date automatically along with your system itself.
Go wild, like deep diving into the terminal, only AFTER you actually arrived and know your way around the Linux Desktop.

I have a very good FLIR brand IR camera from work. I popped the side panel of my pc, and looked at the 12v connector after playing bl4 for an hour and I only saw 60-63c.

text:
Hey Paul. Long-term Linux traditionalist here. I have good reason to respond as someone whoo had used Ubuntu since 2009; anything Canonical isn't _entirely_ bad news, but they've failed to secure Snapcraft while fast-tracking approvals. They did this _twice._ They failed _twice._ Canonical being a Microoft company isn't their third strike, but that's tempting to say as-much; what _really_ got them is during their Unity years, whe their Lenses were sending telemetry data.

Sure, there's Linux Mint which basically amelioerates Ubuntu, but they do that mainly through the use of third-party repositories which may become stale over time, and are basically stuck on Ubuntu LTS, _unless_ you use the _slightly_ older Edge releases which come with a significantly newer kernel… and only comes with one desktop (though, mint-meta-xfce and mint-meta-mate aren't that difficult to install.)

There are immutible distribution, but they basically assume what you have is what's there _always_ which for gamers, may be les-so the case. Adjusting storage topology on select immutible distributons may prove more difficult than otherwise, and _major_ changes in distribution requires replacing _everything._ Not a real major problem, but a real pain in the bandwidth which may provide a less-than-ideal experience for bandwidth-challenged scenarios.

Sure, there's Arch and Gentoo, but those things are outside of reach for most novices and _let'ss face it_ most people won't want to stare at a text console in order to get their Linux journey started. -sure_ there's archinstall but it's still handling everything through text prompts, which is a drag for people who deal best with a graphical interface since that's all they've touched their entire life thanks to either Windows or macOS.

I've resolved _all_ of those problems by going with an Arch-based derivative. Plenty to choose from, but EndeavourOS appears to be one of a few well-developed, and well-curated Arch-based experiences with sensible defaults and considerations for people who are using specific Realtek hardware, with a real knack of _just working_ for many people. For those who willl respond with _But what about CachyOS?_ I said *sane.* Sit. Stay. Good boy. While they've recently reformed themselves into prioritising KDE Plasma, that doesn't stop people from adopting any of the other desktop environments which would be less burdensome on older hardware, and _yes_ for traditionalists like me who thinks _old is gold_ when it comes to foundational software (despite its warts), Xfce is still well-maintained for people seeking a popular GNOME alternative.

Or maybe you _want_ GNOME Shell for its streamlined desktop experience. Perhaps you want to (re-)live the _good old days_ of GNOME 2 as-seen on older Ubuntu; using MATE and building Compiz 0.8 from the AUR with Marco support for gtk-window-decorator. Or you like Mint's desktop, but want it elsewhere; they have that too with Cinnamon. Anything they're supporting can be obtained during installation, and _what software does_ is well laid-out as you select software to use initially.

For package management, EndeavourOS has developed a front-end UI for Reflector called Reflector Simple. Run it, sort by rate for the fastest mirrors and enjoy quicker updates whenever necessary. Yay is the default AUR helper they provide, and you could also use it _just like_ Pacman. If you want a front-end for both, and are using KDE Plasma then Octopi is the thing you want to build. _Else_ if you don't mind using someone else''s work, then Manjaro's Pamac project can be built as an alternative to Pamac, Yay _and_ Octopi; build pamac-gtk3 for the older UI, or pamac-aur for the newer one; both wiill use options defined in libpamac-aur for snap and flatpak suppport if specified before building that.

_Goodness_ that's a lot to type every time. But it's worth the bother for the chance someone learns from it.
